title: Equal Shares
essential_question: How can you show equal parts of a whole in different ways?

pretest.question: A pizza is cut into 4 equal slices. What fraction of the pizza is each slice?
pretest.explanation: 4 equal slices means each slice is 1/4 of the pizza.

pretest.question: Which shape shows equal shares?
choices: A) A circle split into 2 unequal parts
B) A rectangle split into 3 equal parts
C) A triangle with a line near one corner
D) A square split into 2 unequal parts
pretest.explanation: A rectangle split into 3 equal parts shows equal shares. Each part is the same size.

pretest.question: Two friends share a sandwich equally. What fraction does each friend get?
pretest.explanation: Sharing equally between 2 means each gets 1/2.

pretest.question: A cake is shared equally among 6 people. What fraction does each person get?
pretest.explanation: 6 equal shares means each person gets 1/6 of the cake.

pretest.question: 3 friends share 1 granola bar equally. Which shows what each friend gets?
pretest.explanation: 1 bar shared equally among 3 friends gives each friend 1/3.

learn.title: What Are Equal Shares?
learn.content: When you divide a whole into parts that are the same size, you make equal shares. If a pie is cut into 4 equal pieces, each piece is 1/4 of the pie.

learn.title: Different Shapes, Same Fraction
learn.content: You can show 1/2 in many ways: cut a circle down the middle, cut a rectangle across the center, or cut a square diagonally. As long as both parts are the same size, each part is 1/2.

learn.title: Equal vs. Unequal Shares
learn.content: Not every cut makes equal shares. If a rectangle is cut into 3 parts but one part is bigger, those are NOT equal shares. All parts must be the same size to be named as fractions.

unequal.label: NOT equal shares
unequal.description: Rectangle cut unevenly into 3 different-size parts

learn.title: Sixths and Eighths
learn.content: When a whole is divided into 6 equal parts, each part is 1/6 (one sixth). When divided into 8 equal parts, each part is 1/8 (one eighth). The more equal parts you cut, the smaller each part becomes.

learn.title: Naming Equal Shares
learn.content: To name equal shares: (1) Count the total number of equal parts — that is the denominator. (2) Count the parts you are describing — that is the numerator. For 1 out of 6 equal parts, write 1/6.

try.question: A rectangle is split into 8 equal parts. What fraction is each part?
try.explanation: 8 equal parts means each part is 1/8 of the rectangle.
hints: Count the total number of equal parts — that number goes on the bottom of the fraction.
If there are 8 equal parts, each part is 1 out of 8, which is written 1/8.

feedback.correct: Right! 8 equal parts → each part is 1/8.
feedback.incorrect: Count the parts: 8 equal parts means each part is 1/8.

try.question: 4 sisters share 1 pie equally. What fraction does each sister get?
try.explanation: 1 pie shared equally among 4 gives each 1/4.
hints: How many sisters are sharing? That number is the denominator.
1 pie ÷ 4 sisters = 1/4 for each sister.

feedback.correct: Correct! 1 pie shared equally among 4 sisters → each gets 1/4.
feedback.incorrect: There are 4 sisters sharing 1 pie equally → each gets 1/4.

try.question: A shape is divided into 6 equal parts. What fraction is each part?
hints: The total number of equal parts is the denominator.
1 out of 6 equal parts is written as 1/6.

feedback.correct: Yes! 6 equal parts → each part is 1/6.
feedback.incorrect: 6 equal parts means each part is 1 out of 6, written as 1/6.

try.question: How many fourths does it take to make 1 whole?
try.explanation: 4 fourths = 4/4 = 1 whole. You need 4 equal parts.
hints: If each part is 1/4, how many parts do you need to fill the whole?
The denominator tells you how many equal parts make 1 whole: 4 fourths = 1 whole.

feedback.correct: Right! 4 fourths = 4/4 = 1 whole.
feedback.incorrect: You need 4 parts that are each 1/4 to make 1 whole: 4/4 = 1.

try.question: A strip is cut into 6 equal pieces. Which statement is TRUE?
choices: A) Each piece is 1/8 of the strip
B) The pieces are not equal shares
C) Each piece is 1/6 of the strip
D) It takes 8 pieces to make 1 whole
try.explanation: 6 equal pieces → each piece is 1/6. And 6 pieces make 1 whole.
hints: The strip is cut into 6 equal pieces — so the denominator is 6.
1 piece out of 6 equal pieces = 1/6.

feedback.correct: Correct! 6 equal pieces → each is 1/6 of the whole.
feedback.incorrect: There are 6 equal pieces, so each piece is 1/6 of the strip.

practice_r1.question: A cracker is split into 2 equal parts. What fraction is each part?
hints: The cracker is split into 2 equal parts — the denominator is 2.
1 part out of 2 equal parts = 1/2.

feedback.correct: Right! 2 equal parts → each is 1/2.
feedback.incorrect: 2 equal parts means each part is 1 out of 2, written as 1/2.

practice_r1.question: A paper strip is folded into 3 equal parts. What fraction is each part?
hints: 3 equal folds means the denominator is 3.
1 out of 3 equal parts = 1/3.

feedback.correct: Yes! 3 equal folds → each part is 1/3.
feedback.incorrect: 3 equal parts means each is 1/3 of the whole strip.

practice_r1.question: Which shows a circle with equal shares?
choices: A) A circle with a line slightly off-center
B) A circle divided into 4 equal wedges
C) A circle with 3 unequal parts
D) A circle with no lines
practice_r1.explanation: 4 equal wedges means the circle has equal shares.
hints: Equal shares means all parts must be exactly the same size.
Look for the circle where every section is the same size.

feedback.correct: Correct! 4 equal wedges all have the same size — equal shares.
feedback.incorrect: Only the circle with 4 equal wedges has all parts the same size.

practice_r1.question: 6 friends share 1 watermelon equally. What fraction does each friend get?
hints: There are 6 friends sharing equally — the denominator is 6.
1 watermelon divided equally among 6 = 1/6 per friend.

feedback.correct: Right! 6 equal shares → each friend gets 1/6.
feedback.incorrect: Divide 1 watermelon equally among 6 friends → each gets 1/6.

practice_r1.question: A square is divided into 4 equal parts. How many fourths make up the whole square?
hints: The square is divided into fourths. How many fourths are in the whole square?
All 4 equal parts together = 4/4 = 1 whole square.

feedback.correct: Correct! 4 fourths fill the whole square.
feedback.incorrect: 4 equal parts each called fourths: 4/4 = 1 whole.

practice_r1.question: A ribbon is cut into 8 equal pieces. What fraction is each piece?
hints: 8 equal pieces → the denominator is 8.
1 piece out of 8 equal pieces = 1/8.

feedback.correct: Yes! 8 equal pieces → each piece is 1/8.
feedback.incorrect: 8 equal pieces means each piece is 1/8 of the whole ribbon.

practice_r1.question: 2 brothers share 1 bagel equally. What fraction does each brother get?
hints: 2 people sharing equally → the denominator is 2.
1 bagel split into 2 equal halves → each half is 1/2.

feedback.correct: Right! 2 equal shares → each brother gets 1/2.
feedback.incorrect: 1 bagel shared equally between 2 brothers → each gets 1/2.

practice_r1.question: A chocolate bar has 3 equal sections. What fraction is each section?
hints: 3 equal sections → the denominator is 3.
1 out of 3 equal sections = 1/3.

feedback.correct: Correct! 3 equal sections → each is 1/3.
feedback.incorrect: 3 equal sections means each section is 1/3 of the bar.

practice_r1.question: Which fraction name matches a whole divided into 6 equal parts?
practice_r1.explanation: 6 equal parts → each part is called one sixth. The fraction is 1/6.
hints: Match the number of parts to its fraction name: 2=halves, 4=fourths, 6=sixths, 8=eighths.
6 equal parts → the name is sixths.

feedback.correct: Yes! 6 equal parts are called sixths.
feedback.incorrect: Count: 2=halves, 4=fourths, 6=sixths, 8=eighths. 6 parts → sixths.

practice_r1.question: Which shape does NOT show equal shares?
choices: A) A circle cut into 2 equal halves
B) A square cut into 4 equal quarters
C) A rectangle cut into 3 unequal pieces
D) A strip divided into 8 equal parts
practice_r1.explanation: A rectangle cut into 3 unequal pieces does NOT show equal shares — the parts must all be the same size.
hints: Equal shares means every part must be exactly the same size.
Look for the shape where the parts are NOT the same size.

feedback.correct: Right! The rectangle with 3 unequal pieces does NOT have equal shares.
feedback.incorrect: Equal shares requires every part to be the same size. The rectangle with 3 unequal pieces fails that test.

practice_r2.question: A rectangle is divided into parts. 2 parts are big and 1 part is small. Are these equal shares?
choices: A) Yes, because there are 3 parts
B) Yes, because it is a rectangle
C) No, because the parts are not the same size
D) No, because there are only 3 parts
practice_r2.explanation: Equal shares must be the same size. Different-sized parts are not equal shares.
hints: Equal shares means all parts must be the same size — not just any parts.
Are the 3 parts the same size? If not, they are not equal shares.

feedback.correct: Correct! Equal shares require all parts to be the same size.
feedback.incorrect: Having 3 parts is not enough — all parts must be the same size for equal shares.

practice_r2.question: Emma cuts a pan of brownies into 6 equal pieces. She eats 1 piece. What fraction of the brownies did she eat?
practice_r2.explanation: 6 equal pieces → each piece is 1/6. She ate 1 piece = 1/6.
hints: The pan has 6 equal pieces — the denominator is 6.
Emma ate 1 piece out of 6 equal pieces = 1/6.

feedback.correct: Right! 1 piece out of 6 equal pieces = 1/6.
feedback.incorrect: There are 6 equal pieces. Emma ate 1, so she ate 1/6 of the pan.

practice_r2.question: A pie is cut into 8 equal slices. Jake takes 1 slice. What fraction is left?
hints: Start with 8 equal slices. Jake takes 1. How many are left?
8 − 1 = 7 slices remain. Each slice is 1/8, so 7 slices = 7/8.

feedback.correct: Yes! 8 − 1 = 7 slices left → 7/8 of the pie remains.
feedback.incorrect: 8 slices total, Jake takes 1 → 7 left. 7 out of 8 = 7/8.

practice_r2.question: Which two shapes can BOTH show halves?
choices: A) A circle and a triangle
B) A circle and a rectangle
C) A triangle and a pentagon
D) Only circles can show halves
practice_r2.explanation: Both circles and rectangles can be split into 2 equal parts to show halves.
hints: Any shape can show halves if it is divided into exactly 2 equal parts.
Think: can both a circle and a rectangle each be cut into 2 equal parts?

feedback.correct: Correct! Any shape — circle, rectangle, square — can show halves.
feedback.incorrect: Any shape can be cut into 2 equal parts. Both circles and rectangles can show halves.

practice_r2.question: A sub sandwich is cut into 3 equal pieces. Each piece is then cut in half. What fraction of the whole sandwich is each small piece?
practice_r2.explanation: 3 pieces each cut in half = 6 pieces total. Each small piece is 1/6 of the whole.
hints: First cut into 3 equal pieces, then each piece cut in half: 3 × 2 = 6 total pieces.
6 equal pieces → each piece is 1/6 of the whole sandwich.

feedback.correct: Great! 3 pieces cut in half = 6 pieces total → each is 1/6.
feedback.incorrect: 3 pieces × 2 (each cut in half) = 6 pieces. Each small piece = 1/6.

practice_r2.question: Mr. Li cuts a pan into 8 equal pieces. The class eats 3 pieces. What fraction was eaten?
practice_r2.explanation: 3 pieces eaten out of 8 equal pieces = 3/8.
hints: The denominator is 8 (total equal pieces). The numerator is the number eaten.
3 pieces eaten out of 8 total = 3/8.

feedback.correct: Correct! 3 out of 8 equal pieces = 3/8.
feedback.incorrect: 3 pieces were eaten out of 8 equal pieces total → 3/8.

practice_r2.question: A field is divided into 4 equal parts. A farmer plants crops in 1 part. What fraction of the field has NO crops?
practice_r2.explanation: 4 parts − 1 planted = 3 parts with no crops → 3/4.
hints: 4 total parts, 1 has crops. How many parts have no crops?
4 − 1 = 3 parts with no crops. 3 out of 4 = 3/4.

feedback.correct: Right! 4 − 1 = 3 parts with no crops → 3/4 of the field.
feedback.incorrect: 4 total parts − 1 planted = 3 parts with no crops. 3/4 of the field has no crops.

practice_r2.question: A circle is cut into 6 equal wedges. 1 wedge is shaded. Which correctly names the shaded part?
hints: Count the total equal wedges — that is the denominator.
1 shaded wedge out of 6 equal wedges = 1/6.

feedback.correct: Yes! 1 out of 6 equal parts = 1/6.
feedback.incorrect: The circle has 6 equal wedges and 1 is shaded → 1/6.

practice_r2.question: Lily has a granola bar cut into 4 equal pieces. She eats 1 piece. Which fraction shows what is LEFT?
practice_r2.explanation: 4 pieces − 1 eaten = 3 remaining → 3/4 of the bar is left.
hints: 4 pieces total, 1 eaten. How many are left?
4 − 1 = 3 pieces remaining. 3 out of 4 equal pieces = 3/4.

feedback.correct: Correct! 4 − 1 = 3 pieces left → 3/4 of the bar remains.
feedback.incorrect: 4 pieces total, 1 eaten → 3 left. 3 out of 4 = 3/4.

practice_r2.question: Shape A is a circle cut into 2 equal halves. Shape B is a rectangle cut into 2 equal halves. Do both shapes show the same fraction?
choices: B) No, they are different shapes
C) No, circles and rectangles cannot show the same fraction
D) Yes, but only if they are the same size
practice_r2.explanation: Both shapes are divided into 2 equal parts, so both show 1/2 — the fraction is the same regardless of shape.
hints: The fraction depends on the number of equal parts, not on the shape.
Both shapes are cut into 2 equal parts. 1 out of 2 = 1/2 for both.

feedback.correct: Right! Any shape divided into 2 equal parts shows 1/2.
feedback.incorrect: Both shapes are cut into 2 equal parts. The fraction 1/2 does not depend on the shape.

practice_r3.question: 3 children share 2 sandwiches equally. How much does each child get?
hints: Divide the total (2 sandwiches) by the number of children (3).
2 ÷ 3 means each child gets 2/3 of a sandwich.

feedback.correct: Correct! 2 sandwiches shared equally among 3 → each gets 2/3.
feedback.incorrect: 2 sandwiches ÷ 3 children = 2/3 per child.

practice_r3.question: Tom says a square cut into 4 parts always shows fourths. Is he correct?
choices: A) Yes, 4 parts always means fourths
B) No, the 4 parts must be equal to be fourths
C) Yes, squares always have equal parts
D) No, squares cannot show fourths
practice_r3.explanation: Just having 4 parts is not enough. The parts must be equal in size to be called fourths.
hints: The word 'fourths' means 4 equal parts — both conditions are needed.
Could you cut a square into 4 parts that are NOT equal? If yes, those are not fourths.

feedback.correct: Right! You need 4 parts AND they must all be equal size.
feedback.incorrect: 4 parts are required, but they must also be equal in size to be called fourths.

practice_r3.question: A granola bar is shared equally among 4 friends, then each friend cuts their piece in half. How many equal pieces are there in all?
practice_r3.explanation: 4 pieces, each cut in half = 4 × 2 = 8 equal pieces. Each piece is 1/8 of the whole bar.
hints: Start with 4 equal pieces. Each piece is then cut in half.
4 pieces × 2 (each cut in half) = 8 pieces total.

feedback.correct: Yes! 4 × 2 = 8 equal pieces. Each is 1/8 of the whole bar.
feedback.incorrect: 4 pieces each cut in half: 4 × 2 = 8 total pieces, each = 1/8.

practice_r3.question: 4 students each eat 1/4 of a pizza. Together, how much pizza did they eat?
hints: Each student eats 1/4. Add up 4 students: 1/4 + 1/4 + 1/4 + 1/4 = ?
4 pieces of 1/4 each = 4/4 = 1 whole pizza.

feedback.correct: Correct! 4 × (1/4) = 4/4 = 1 whole pizza.
feedback.incorrect: 4 students × 1/4 each = 4/4 = 1 whole pizza.

practice_r3.question: Kenji shades some parts of a rectangle divided into 8 equal parts. His friend says 'You shaded 3/8.' How many parts did Kenji shade?
practice_r3.explanation: 3/8 means 3 out of 8 equal parts. Kenji shaded 3 parts.
hints: The numerator (top number) tells you how many parts are shaded.
In 3/8, the numerator is 3 → 3 parts are shaded.

feedback.correct: Right! 3/8 → the numerator 3 means 3 parts were shaded.
feedback.incorrect: The numerator of 3/8 is 3. Kenji shaded 3 parts out of 8.
